claim_chain: Sugary drink taxes reduce sales in supermarkets but their impact in restaurants is unknown
We analyzed millions of transactions from fast food restaurants in five jurisdictions with taxes
Overall, taxes were not associated with meaningful changes in beverage calories purchased
Oakland showed reductions in combo meal beverage calories; Philadelphia showed total calorie reductions
Results suggest current tax sizes may be too small to change restaurant purchases
Restaurant consumers may differ from retail consumers in their response to taxes

typical_pattern: State main finding with quantitative result
Compare to prior literature or other jurisdictions
Connect to broader implications or policy relevance

likely_priorities: Completeness of transaction data (in-store vs drive-through)
Validity of synthetic control matching approach
Representativeness of Cook County sample (47 of 60 restaurants)
likely_attacks: Exclusion of 30% of transactions (in-store) limits conclusions
Majority of sample from Cook County where tax was repealed after 4 months
Cannot observe demographic characteristics of purchasers
Quasi-experimental design may have unobserved confounding
Results may not generalize to other restaurant types or jurisdictions
reviewer_preference_profile.inference_basis: The paper explicitly states 'A key limitation of our study was the exclusion of beverage calorie data from in-store transactions (~30% of the sample)' and 'The majority of the restaurants in our sample (47 out of 60) were located in Cook County, where the tax was repealed after 4 months'. It acknowledges 'it is possible that unobserved characteristics of comparison restaurants were correlated with sugary drink tax implementation' and 'our data may not be generalizable to all restaurant settings or jurisdictions'. The authors also note 'We do not know the demographic characteristics of the person(s) who made the transactions'. These self-identified limitations form the basis for inferring likely reviewer concerns.

phrase_evidence.phrase: We found that the implementation of sugary drink taxes in fast food restaurants in five U.S. jurisdictions was not associated with meaningful changes in beverage calories purchased over time
In contrast, a systematic review reported that sugary drink taxes are associated with a ~15% reduction in sugary drink sales in supermarkets
potentially due to differences in baseline consumption, social norms, or other unmeasured population-level characteristics
customers ordered ~14% fewer beverage calories per transaction from combo meals
A key limitation of our study was the exclusion of beverage calorie data from in-store transactions
it is possible that unobserved characteristics of comparison restaurants were correlated with sugary drink tax implementation
our data may not be generalizable to all restaurant settings or jurisdictions
suggests that the sizes of sugary drink taxes in the U.S. are too small to change beverage calories purchased in fast food restaurants
provide evidence that customers may change their non-beverage purchases after tax implementation
Our results are consistent with the one other study assessing the impact of sugary drink taxes on sales from restaurant purchases
In the absence of randomization, our quasi-experimental approach was rigorous and accounted for secular trends over time
we maintain that our limitations are an acceptable trade-off of analyzing a detailed set of objective data from millions of transactions
The findings from our quasi-experimental study are valuable in that we used millions of transactions across five jurisdictions
We used six years of transaction-level sales data from 7,341 Taco Bell restaurant locations
We used a difference-in-differences design with calendar month and restaurant fixed effects
Overall, we observed no association between taxes and changes in beverage calories per transaction
our findings suggest that sugary drink taxes may not be effective in reducing beverage calorie consumption in fast food restaurants
a signaling effect may have also shifted customers to purchase meals with lower overall calories
restaurant consumers may be less likely to travel to other jurisdictions for a single meal
We cannot observe whether larger taxes would be more effective using real-world data
The vertical dashed line represents the date of repeal for Cook County, IL
Conditional on a transaction including a beverage item
Unconditional on a transaction including a beverage item
This provides additional evidence to suggest that
That said, it is possible that

verification_status.notes: All extracted phrases are literal substrings from the input text. No external references, invented numbers, or author names were included. The profile is based entirely on the provided abstract, discussion, and figure legends.
